_comment_2: PortPane by ShackDesk — USB Device Database
_comment_4: Author   : Mark McDow (N4TEK) — My Computer Guru LLC
_comment_9: This file is the known USB device database for PortPane. When PortPane
_comment_10: detects a USB serial or audio device, it looks up the VID and PID here
_comment_11: to identify the device, suggest a baud rate, and decide whether it is
_comment_12: a radio interface. Community contributions of new device entries are
_comment_13: very welcome — you do not need to know how to code.
_comment_16: This file must be valid JSON at all times. A single misplaced comma or
_comment_17: missing quote will prevent PortPane from loading any device data.
_comment_18: Before submitting a change, validate your JSON at: https://jsonlint.com
_comment_19: Common mistakes: trailing comma on last item, unescaped backslash in text.
_comment_22: The entry below shows every field. Copy it as a starting point:
_comment_31: "notes": "Common on DigiRig and many radio interface clones"
_comment_35: vid            : USB Vendor ID  — 4 hex characters, uppercase (e.g. "10C4")
_comment_36: pid            : USB Product ID — 4 hex characters, uppercase (e.g. "EA60")
_comment_37: name           : Human-readable device name shown in PortPane UI
_comment_39: radio_interface: true if this device is used with amateur or professional
_comment_40: radio equipment; false for general-purpose adapters
_comment_41: baud_hint      : Suggested default baud rate as a number (e.g. 9600,
_comment_42: 115200), or null if not applicable (audio devices)
_comment_44: notes          : Brief plain-English description of the device or chipset.
_comment_45: Include known-compatible radio models if applicable.
_comment_47: HOW TO FIND VID AND PID ON WINDOWS
_comment_48: Step 1: Connect your USB device to the computer.
_comment_49: Step 2: Right-click the Start button and choose 'Device Manager'.
_comment_50: Step 3: Expand 'Ports (COM & LPT)' or 'Sound, video and game controllers'.
_comment_51: Step 4: Right-click your device and choose 'Properties'.
_comment_53: Step 6: In the 'Property' dropdown, choose 'Hardware IDs'.
_comment_54: Step 7: Look for a value like: USB\VID_10C4&PID_EA60
_comment_55: The 4 characters after VID_ are your VID (e.g. 10C4).
_comment_56: The 4 characters after PID_ are your PID (e.g. EA60).
_comment_57: Step 8: Add an entry to this file using those values (uppercase).
_comment_60: To add a device: open a 'USB Device Database Addition' issue on GitHub,
_comment_61: or submit a pull request editing this file directly.
_comment_63: Please validate your JSON before submitting: https://jsonlint.com

devices.name: C-Media USB Audio CODEC
devices.notes: Common generic USB CODEC chipset used in many radio interfaces

devices.name: C-Media CM108 USB Audio
devices.notes: CM108 chipset — found in SignaLink and RIGblaster clones

devices.name: C-Media CM119 USB Audio
devices.notes: CM119/CM119A chipset — common in radio-grade USB CODECs

devices.name: FTDI FT232R USB UART
devices.notes: Very common rig control and PTT cable chipset

devices.name: Silicon Labs CP2102 USB Serial
devices.notes: Common on DigiRig and many radio interface clones

devices.name: Silicon Labs CP2108 Quad USB Serial
devices.notes: Found in Icom IC-7300 and IC-705 direct USB

devices.name: CH340 USB Serial
devices.notes: Common on Arduino clones and cheap USB cables

devices.name: Prolific PL2303 USB Serial
devices.notes: Very common USB-serial adapter; not radio-specific

devices.name: Tigertronics SignaLink USB
devices.notes: SignaLink USB audio interface — very common in digital modes
